verses.english_translation: Having created it, he entered into that very creation. Having entered, he became both the defined and the undefined.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तत् सृष्ट्वा — जगत् उत्पाद्य — तत् एव अनुप्राविशत् — जगत् अन्तः प्रविशत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Having created the universe, Brahman entered into it as the inner Self of all beings. Brahman is both transcendent (beyond creation) and immanent (within creation). This is the teaching of divine immanence.

verses.transliteration: ahaṃ brahmāsmi ||1.4.10||
verses.english_translation: I am Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अहम् — प्रत्यगात्मा — ब्रह्म — परमात्मा — अस्मि — अभिन्नः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: 'I am Brahman' — the individual Self is identical with the supreme Brahman. This mahavakya of the Shukla Yajurveda declares the non-difference of the individual and the universal Self.

verses.sanskrit: नेति नेति | अगृह्यो न हि गृह्यते | अशीर्यो न हि शीर्यते | असङ्गो न हि सज्यते | असितो न व्यथते | न रिष्यति ||३.९.२६||
verses.transliteration: neti neti | agṛhyo na hi gṛhyate | aśīryo na hi śīryate | asaṅgo na hi sajyate | asito na vyathate | na riṣyati ||3.9.26||
verses.english_translation: Not this, not this. It is ungraspable, for it cannot be grasped. It is indestructible, for it does not decay. It is unattached, it does not suffer, it is not injured.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नेति नेति — सर्वविशेषनिषेधेन ब्रह्म निरूप्यते। यत् किञ्चित् उपाधिः — तत् ब्रह्म न।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: By the method of 'not this, not this,' Brahman is described by negating all limiting adjuncts. Whatever can be pointed to as 'this' — that is not Brahman. Brahman is the pure witness beyond all objectification.

verses.sanskrit: न वा अरे पत्युः कामाय पतिः प्रियो भवति | आत्मनस्तु कामाय पतिः प्रियो भवति ||२.४.५||
verses.transliteration: na vā are patyuḥ kāmāya patiḥ priyo bhavati | ātmanas tu kāmāya patiḥ priyo bhavati ||2.4.5||

word_meanings.meaning: for the sake of the Self

verses.english_translation: Verily, it is not for the husband's sake that the husband is dear, but for the sake of the Self that the husband is dear.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: पत्युः कामाय — पत्युः स्वार्थाय — पतिः प्रियः न भवति। आत्मनः कामाय — स्वस्य आत्मनः प्रीत्यर्थम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Yajnavalkya teaches Maitreyi that all love is ultimately love of the Self. The husband, wife, children, wealth — all are dear not for their own sake but because the Self shines through them. The Self alone is the ultimate object of all love.

verses.sanskrit: आत्मा वा अरे द्रष्टव्यः श्रोतव्यो मन्तव्यो निदिध्यासितव्यः ||२.४.५||
verses.transliteration: ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ ||2.4.5||
verses.english_translation: Verily, the Self should be seen, heard, reflected upon, and deeply meditated upon.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आत्मा — द्रष्टव्यः — श्रोतव्यः — मन्तव्यः — निदिध्यासितव्यः — चतुर्विधः साधनक्रमः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The four-fold method of Self-realization: shravana (hearing the teaching), manana (reflecting on it), nididhyasana (deep meditation), and sakshatkara (direct vision). This is the complete path of Vedanta.

verses.sanskrit: यत्र हि द्वैतमिव भवति तदितर इतरं पश्यति | यत्र त्वस्य सर्वमात्मैवाभूत्तत्केन कं पश्येत् ||२.४.१४||
verses.transliteration: yatra hi dvaitam iva bhavati tad itara itaraṃ paśyati | yatra tv asya sarvam ātmaivābhūt tat kena kaṃ paśyet ||2.4.14||

word_meanings.meaning: where everything has become the Self
by what and whom would one see

verses.english_translation: Where there is duality, one sees the other. But where everything has become the Self — by what and whom would one see?
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: यत्र द्वैतम् — तत्र इतरः इतरम् पश्यति। यत्र सर्वम् आत्मा — तत्र केन कम् पश्येत् — द्रष्टृदृश्यभेदः नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: In duality, the subject sees the object. But in the non-dual Self, there is no subject-object distinction. By what instrument would one see, and what would one see? The seer, the seeing, and the seen are all one Self.

verses.sanskrit: विज्ञानमानन्दं ब्रह्म | रातिर्दातुः परायणम् ||३.९.२८||
verses.transliteration: vijñānam ānandaṃ brahma | rātir dātuḥ parāyaṇam ||3.9.28||

word_meanings.meaning: the ultimate goal of the giver

verses.english_translation: Brahman is consciousness and bliss — the ultimate goal of the giver.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: विज्ञानम् — चैतन्यम्। आनन्दम् — परमानन्दम्। ब्रह्म — एतत् स्वरूपम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Brahman is consciousness (vijnana) and bliss (ananda). This is the definition of Brahman in the Brihadaranyaka — pure consciousness that is also pure bliss. The giver of knowledge attains this as the ultimate goal.

verses.sanskrit: सर्वं ह्येतद्ब्रह्म | अयमात्मा ब्रह्म | सोऽयमात्मा चतुष्पात् ||२.५.१९||
verses.transliteration: sarvaṃ hy etad brahma | ayam ātmā brahma | so'yam ātmā catuṣpāt ||2.5.19||
verses.english_translation: All this is indeed Brahman. This Self is Brahman. This same Self has four quarters.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सर्वम् एतत् ब्रह्म — जगत् ब्रह्मैव। अयम् आत्मा ब्रह्म — प्रत्यगात्मा परब्रह्मैव।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: All this universe is Brahman alone. This inner Self is the supreme Brahman. The four quarters are the four states: waking, dreaming, deep sleep, and the fourth (turiya).

verses.sanskrit: यो वेद निहितं गुहायां परमे व्योमन् | सोऽश्नुते सर्वान् कामान् सह ब्रह्मणा विपश्चितेति ||१.४.१||
verses.transliteration: yo veda nihitaṃ guhāyāṃ parame vyoman | so'śnute sarvān kāmān saha brahmaṇā vipaściteti ||1.4.1||

word_meanings.meaning: hidden in the cave of the heart

verses.english_translation: One who knows it hidden in the cave of the heart, in the supreme ether, attains all desires together with the all-knowing Brahman.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: गुहायाम् — हृदयाकाशे — निहितम् — ब्रह्म — वेद — सः सर्वान् कामान् अश्नुते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: One who knows Brahman hidden in the cave of the heart attains all desires — not by fulfilling individual desires but by becoming Brahman, which is the fulfillment of all desires.

verses.sanskrit: तद्यथा पुष्करपलाश आपो न श्लिष्यन्त एवमेवंविदि पापं कर्म न श्लिष्यते ||४.३.१४||
verses.transliteration: tad yathā puṣkarapalāśa āpo na śliṣyante evam evaṃvidi pāpaṃ karma na śliṣyate ||4.3.14||

word_meanings.meaning: water does not cling to a lotus leaf

verses.english_translation: Just as water does not cling to a lotus leaf, so evil karma does not cling to one who knows this.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: पुष्करपलाशे — कमलपत्रे — आपः न श्लिष्यन्ते। एवम् — एवंविदि — पापम् कर्म न श्लिष्यते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as water rolls off a lotus leaf without wetting it, so karma — both good and bad — does not cling to the knower of Brahman. The knower is beyond the reach of karma because he has transcended the ego that accumulates karma.

verses.sanskrit: यत्र नान्यत्पश्यति नान्यच्छृणोति नान्यद्विजानाति स भूमा | अथ यत्रान्यत्पश्यत्यन्यच्छृणोत्यन्यद्विजानाति तदल्पम् ||७.२४.१||
verses.transliteration: yatra nānyat paśyati nānyac chṛṇoti nānyad vijānāti sa bhūmā | atha yatrānyat paśyaty anyac chṛṇoty anyad vijānāti tad alpam ||7.24.1||
verses.english_translation: Where one sees nothing else, hears nothing else, knows nothing else — that is the Infinite. Where one sees something else, hears something else, knows something else — that is the finite.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: यत्र नान्यत् पश्यति — एकत्वदर्शनम् — स भूमा — ब्रह्म। यत्र अन्यत् पश्यति — द्वैतदर्शनम् — तत् अल्पम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Infinite is where there is no 'other' — no duality. The finite is where there is an 'other.' This is the definition of Brahman as the Infinite (Bhuma) — the state of non-dual awareness where there is nothing outside oneself.

verses.transliteration: ekam evādvitīyam ||6.2.1||
verses.english_translation: One alone, without a second.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एकम् — अद्वितीयम् — ब्रह्म — द्वितीयरहितम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Brahman is one alone, without a second. Before creation, only Brahman existed. This is the foundation of Advaita Vedanta — the non-dual nature of ultimate reality.

verses.sanskrit: तत्सृष्ट्वा तदेवानुप्राविशत् | तदनुप्रविश्य सच्च त्यच्चाभवत् ||२.६.१||
verses.transliteration: tat sṛṣṭvā tad evānuprāviśat | tad anupraviśya sac ca tyac cābhavat ||2.6.1||

word_meanings.meaning: both the defined and the undefined

verses.english_translation: Having created it, he entered into that very creation. Having entered, he became both the defined and the undefined.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तत् सृष्ट्वा — जगत् उत्पाद्य — तत् एव अनुप्राविशत् — जगत् अन्तः प्रविशत्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Having created the universe, Brahman entered into it as the inner Self of all beings. Brahman is both transcendent (beyond creation) and immanent (within creation). This is the teaching of divine immanence.

verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अहम् — प्रत्यगात्मा — ब्रह्म — परमात्मा — अस्मि — अभिन्नः।

verses.english_translation: Not this, not this. It is ungraspable, for it cannot be grasped. It is indestructible, for it does not decay. It is unattached, it does not suffer, it is not injured.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नेति नेति — सर्वविशेषनिषेधेन ब्रह्म निरूप्यते। यत् किञ्चित् उपाधिः — तत् ब्रह्म न।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: By the method of 'not this, not this,' Brahman is described by negating all limiting adjuncts. Whatever can be pointed to as 'this' — that is not Brahman. Brahman is the pure witness beyond all objectification.

verses.sanskrit: न वा अरे पत्युः कामाय पतिः प्रियो भवति | आत्मनस्तु कामाय पतिः प्रियो भवति ||२.४.५||
verses.transliteration: na vā are patyuḥ kāmāya patiḥ priyo bhavati | ātmanas tu kāmāya patiḥ priyo bhavati ||2.4.5||

word_meanings.meaning: for the sake of the Self

verses.english_translation: Verily, it is not for the husband's sake that the husband is dear, but for the sake of the Self that the husband is dear.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: पत्युः कामाय — पत्युः स्वार्थाय — पतिः प्रियः न भवति। आत्मनः कामाय — स्वस्य आत्मनः प्रीत्यर्थम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Yajnavalkya teaches Maitreyi that all love is ultimately love of the Self. The husband, wife, children, wealth — all are dear not for their own sake but because the Self shines through them. The Self alone is the ultimate object of all love.
